description: 输入客单价和月单量，对比 PayPal 与 Stripe 的月手续费总额和有效费率。
excerpt: PayPal 和 Stripe 在标准卡交易上都收大约 3% + $0.30，但一旦叠加国际卡、货币转换、拒付费，结构差异立刻拉开。一个 $35 客单价 × 300 单/月的店铺，月流水 $10,50
body: PayPal 和 Stripe 在标准卡交易上都收大约 3% + $0.30，但一旦叠加国际卡、货币转换、拒付费，结构差异立刻拉开。一个 $35 客单价 × 300 单/月的店铺，月流水 $10,500——1% 有效费率差就是 $1,260/年。 计算器怎么算 客单价 (USD)——平均订单金额。 月单量——每月订单总数。 输出： 各家月手续费 有效费率 = 月手续费 ÷ 月流水 差额——按月的金额差 默认假设 PayPal 商业交易（4.4% + $0.30）和 Stripe 标准卡（2.9% + $0.30 基础，含约 1.5% 跨境卡附加作为综合有效费率）。按自己的实际客单组成调整。 不同订单量的有效费率 客单价 月单量 流水 PayPal 费 Stripe 费 月差 年差 $25 200 $5,000 $280 $194 $86 $1,032 $35 300 $10,500 $569 $404 $165 $1,980 $60 500 $30,000 $1,590 $1,140 $450 $5,400 $120 150 $18,000 $948 $696 $252 $3,024 差额与流水大致线性相关，与单数无关。$30k/月流水，PayPal 多收 $5,400/年——在某些市场够一个全职员工。Stripe 在标准国内卡场景胜出；PayPal 只在买家明确拒绝刷卡的场景才赢。 这工具不算什么 拒付 / 争议费（每笔 $15–20）——预留准备金。 订阅计费折扣——Stripe Billing 和 PayPal Subscriptions 的留存卡定价不一样。 本地支付（iDEAL、Klarna、Alipay）——Adyen、Mollie、Airwallex Payment Acceptance 等聚合器用一笔费率包打。 刷卡 vs 输卡——POS 与线上定价不同。 常见错误 把"无月费"等同于免费——PayPal 无月费但单笔费率更高，跑数看看。 忽略 1.5% 国际附加费——在基础费率上叠加。PayPal 跨境交易 4.4% + 1.5% = 5.9%，还没算汇损。 忘记货币转换——客户付 EUR、你结算 USD，PayPal 在公示汇率上再加 3–4%。Stripe 是 1%。 低估拒付——准备 0.1–0.3% 流水的准备金；高客单品类（电子、保健品）可能到 1%。 何时切换服务商 <$50k/月 + 纯国内：Stripe 通常便宜 0.5–1%。PayPal 的支票罕见。 跨境销售：Stripe 银行间汇率比 PayPal 内嵌汇损便宜 1–2%。 B2B 或年长欧盟客户：Stripe 为主，PayPal 留为备用——结账转化常常提升。 订阅业务：Stripe Billing 的留存卡费率显著低于 PayPal Subscriptions（规

title: PayPal vs Stripe Fee Comparator
description: Compare monthly PayPal and Stripe processing fees for your unit price and order volume. See which is cheaper.
excerpt: Both PayPal and Stripe charge roughly 3% + $0.30 on standard card transactions, but the structures d
body: Both PayPal and Stripe charge roughly 3% + $0.30 on standard card transactions, but the structures diverge quickly once you add international cards, currency conversion, and dispute fees. A $35 product on 300 orders/month generates $10,500 in revenue — a 1% effective-rate difference is $1,260/year. How this calculator works Unit price (USD) — average order value. Monthly volume — total orders per month. The tool returns: Monthly fee for each provider Effective rate = monthly fee ÷ monthly revenue Wins by — the dollar gap per month Defaults assume PayPal commercial transactions (4.4% + $0.30) and Stripe standard card (2.9% + $0.30 base, with an estimated 1.5% cross-border card surcharge baked into Stripe's effective rate for international orders). Adjust the inputs to your actual mix. Effective fee across order volumes Unit price Monthly orders Revenue PayPal fee Stripe fee Gap / month Gap / year $25 200 $5,000 $280 $194 $86 $1,032 $35 300 $10,500 $569 $404 $165 $1,980 $60 500 $30,000 $1,590 $1,140 $450 $5,400 $120 150 $18,000 $948 $696 $252 $3,024 The gap scales roughly linearly with revenue, not with order count. At $30k/month, PayPal's premium is $5,400/year — that's a full-time

title: PayPal vs Stripe Fees in 2026: The Real Cost Per Sale
description: Side-by-side fee comparison of PayPal and Stripe for cross-border sellers. Includes micro-fee trap, chargeback handling, and currency conversion costs.

title: PayPal World 与跨境钱包：微信支付 + UPI + Mercado Pago 互通，对 SMB 卖家到底改了什么
description: 一条高互动 X 帖（770 赞、1000+ 转推）报道了 PayPal 推出 PayPal World，把微信支付、印度 UPI、拉美 Mercado Pago 接进一个跨境钱包。下面拆这个互通实际做了什么、对 SMB 卖家改了什么、新闻稿没点名的失败模式。
excerpt: 一条高互动 X 帖（770 赞、1000+ 转推）报道了 PayPal 推出 PayPal World，把微信支付、印度 UPI、拉美 Mercado Pago 接进一个跨境钱包。标题场景是中国游客用
body: 一条高互动 X 帖（770 赞、1000+ 转推）报道了 PayPal 推出 PayPal World，把微信支付、印度 UPI、拉美 Mercado Pago 接进一个跨境钱包。标题场景是中国游客用 PayPal 扫微信支付二维码，或美国游客用 PayPal 扫 Mercado Pago 二维码。下面拆这个互通实际做了什么、对 SMB 卖家改了什么、新闻稿没点名的失败模式。 四个钱包互通实际做了什么 钱包 区域 2026 年日活 在 PayPal World 中的角色 PayPal 全球 200M+ 锚定钱包 微信支付 中国大陆 800M+ 出境游客覆盖 印度 UPI 印度 400M+ 入境游客覆盖 + 境内汇款 Mercado Pago 拉美 150M+ 拉美出境游客覆盖 这四个区域的任一个 PayPal 用户都可以扫其他三个的二维码。商户端按本地钱包收款，无需自己接入那个钱包。跨境换汇由 PayPal 在中间处理。 对 SMB 卖家改了什么 三个具体变化： 「我没有微信支付账号」的反对消失了。 一个中国游客走进美国纪念品店，可以扫店家 PayPal 二维码用微信支付付款，按人民币付。店家按美元收。这是中国游客集中的零售里被引用最多的摩擦点。 卖家通过现有接入收款。 没有新的商户入驻流程、没有超越跨境溢价的新费率表、没有新的合规审查。变化在钱包侧，不在商户侧。 结账页可以给游客提供「用你的本地钱包付」。 一个有印度游客客群的美国商家可以提供「用 UPI 付」而无需自己接入 UPI——PayPal World 桥接。 每笔交易的经济学 成本项 费率 备注 PayPal 境内商户费 2.9% + $0.30 标准卡费率 PayPal 跨境溢价 +1.5%（典型） 货币不同时适用 钱包到钱包汇兑差 1–3%（历史） PayPal 在换汇上吃 实际跨境钱包费率 约 5.4–7.4% + $0.30 对比境内 2.9% + $0.30 钱包到钱包费率大约是境内卡费率的两倍。对净利润只有 3–8% 的 SMB 卖家，这是 make-or-break 的那一项。新闻稿把互通框成「不增收新费」事件；跨境溢价才是成本。 失败模式 通过外国钱包的争议。 外国客户通过本国钱包发起争议，PayPal 仲裁。卖家比境内卡争议的胜诉机会小，因为争议路径是钱包专属的。跨境钱包交易的标准争议率比境内卡交易高 1.5–3 倍。 退款摩擦。 钱包到钱包的退款走同一个钱包，反向再吃一次汇兑差。一笔 $50 USD 退给中国微信支付客户，真实成本可能 $51–52（计反向汇兑差）。 受制裁走廊的合规。 在受制裁走廊（例如中国大陆 ↔ 美国某些商户类目）的钱包到钱包交易可能被拦截，即使两边钱包技术上都是支持的。卖家看到一笔支付失败但没有明确原因。 带走就一句 PayPal World 砍掉了一个

title: PayPal World and the cross-border wallet: what the WeChat Pay + UPI + Mercado Pago integration actually changes for SMB sellers
description: A high-engagement X post (770 likes, 1,000+ reposts) covered PayPal''s launch of PayPal World, integrating WeChat Pay, India UPI, and Latin America''s Mercado Pago into a single cross-border wallet. A read of what the integration actually does, what it changes for SMB sellers, and the failure modes the announcement does not name.
excerpt: A high-engagement X post (770 likes, 1,000+ reposts) covered PayPal's launch of PayPal World, integr
body: A high-engagement X post (770 likes, 1,000+ reposts) covered PayPal's launch of PayPal World, integrating WeChat Pay, India UPI, and Latin America's Mercado Pago into a single cross-border wallet. The headline use case is a Chinese tourist using PayPal to scan a WeChat Pay QR, or a U.S. tourist using PayPal to scan a Mercado Pago QR. What follows is a read of what the integration actually does, what it changes for SMB sellers, and the failure modes the announcement does not name. What the four-wallet integration actually does Wallet Region Daily-active users (2026) Role in PayPal World PayPal Global 200M+ Anchor wallet WeChat Pay Mainland China 800M+ Outbound-tourist coverage India UPI India 400M+ Inbound-tourist coverage + domestic remittance Mercado Pago Latin America 150M+ Outbound-tourist coverage from LatAm A PayPal user in any of those four regions can scan a QR from any of the other three. The merchant receives payment in their home wallet without needing to integrate that wallet directly. The cross-border conversion is handled by PayPal in the middle. What it changes for an SMB seller Three concrete changes: The "I do not have a WeChat Pay account" objection disappears. A C

title: Postiz 案例:30+ 社交渠道 + 开源,撑起 14.5 万美金 MRR,跟主流赛道错开的那条路
description: 拆解 Postiz(开源社媒排期工具)走到 14.5 万美金 MRR 的路子。它真正变现的是什么,护城河在哪里,案例没说清楚哪几件事,以及「长尾集成 + 开源」单位经济学的拆解。
excerpt: 一个知识星球帖子抛出一个常见误区：做工具型产品，上来就想做一个「更好用的版本」，结果掉进最挤的赛道跟现有玩家抢同一批客户。案例拆的是一款独立开发者做的开源社媒排期工具，据报已跨过 14.5 万美金 M
body: 一个知识星球帖子抛出一个常见误区：做工具型产品，上来就想做一个「更好用的版本」，结果掉进最挤的赛道跟现有玩家抢同一批客户。案例拆的是一款独立开发者做的开源社媒排期工具，据报已跨过 14.5 万美金 MRR，做法是接 30+ 发布渠道，包括主流工具懒得做的长尾平台。下面是数字、承重部分，以及案例没说的事。 案例描述的内容 数量 值(自报) 产品 Postiz(开源社媒排期) 公开 MRR 14.5 万美金 集成数 30+(主流 + Mastodon、Bluesky、Nostr、Warpcast、Telegram、Discord 等长尾) 许可证 开源 + hosted 付费订阅 团队规模 单个独立开发者(按案例口吻) 数字由创始人自报，作为方向性参考；案例用它们去论证「开源 + 长尾」的组合是承重策略，不是功能集。 为什么 30+ 集成是护城河 功能型护城河（更聪明的 UI、更干净的 dashboard、更精致的模板）半衰期只有几周。下一个版本竞品就追上来了，差异化消失。 集成型护城河累积而不衰减： 每加一个集成，带来三样东西： 新买家池。 每个集成都是一个长尾搜索词（「post to Mastodon + scheduler」)。SEO 自然增长，不烧钱。 涨价锚点。 老订阅者看到 dashboard 里不断多出来的新集成，「同等月费下价值上升」，留存自动加固，不需要急着提价。 迁移税。 一旦客户把 20+ 渠道都接进了某个排期器，评估竞品时迁移代价（重新走 20 遍 OAuth、配权限、回放队列）是真实的。切换成本随集成宽度增长。 到了 30+ 集成，竞品 90 天内追不上来。这才是护城河。 为什么开源是获客渠道 案例把开源框架解释成免费获客。三套机制： GitHub star 当 SEO。 1 万–2 万 star 的仓库，自然排在 「social scheduler github」 搜索上，每一个 star 都是一条被动外链，流向自部署的漏斗。营销预算接近零。 自部署者当布道者。 一家小代理自己部署 Postiz、服务 5 个客户，就是一个 5 家客户的推荐漏斗。不会主动投放，但会跟同行说。漏斗的获客成本是这位执行安装的人，不是付费投放。 贡献者当集成工程师。 Mastodon 集成需要 patch 时，这是个开源工件，会有社区贡献者接手，而不是创始人自己背带宽。在不发工资的前提下，工程带宽靠社区放大。 开源 / 不开源的选择，本质上是营销动作。Hosted 计划才是钱，自部署是漏斗。 14.5 万美金 MRR 的算术 对一个独立 SaaS，这个数字落在顶级分布。这个品类的大多数独立工具一辈子没跨过 2–3 万 MRR。14.5 万这个数，意味着这位独立开发者要么（a）花了 4 年以上死磕集成宽度，(b）拿到了能压过按席位数学的企业 / 代理 tier

title: Postiz 单人做到 14.5 万美金 MRR:集成 30 个别人懒得接的平台才是护城河
description: 一支单人开发的社媒排期工具,据披露月经常性收入做到 14.5 万美金,秘诀是接了 30 多个发布渠道——包括竞品懒得接的 Mastodon、Bluesky、Nostr、Warpcast、Telegram、Discord。下面拆「渠道数」为什么比「更好的 UI」更重要,以及开源怎么变成付费转化漏斗。
excerpt: 一支单人开发的社媒排期工具 Postiz 据披露月经常性收入做到 14.5 万美金。产品不是靠 UI 精致或功能深度赢的。它赢在「接了 30 多个发布渠道」——包括竞品觉得「用户太少不值得做」的 Ma
body: 一支单人开发的社媒排期工具 Postiz 据披露月经常性收入做到 14.5 万美金。产品不是靠 UI 精致或功能深度赢的。它赢在「接了 30 多个发布渠道」——包括竞品觉得「用户太少不值得做」的 Mastodon、Bluesky、Nostr、Warpcast、Telegram、Discord。整套产品还开源。下面拆「渠道数」为什么是护城河,以及开源怎么变成付费转化漏斗。 数字 数量 值 MRR(最新) 14.5 万美金 推算 ARR 174 万美金 发布渠道 30+ 代码许可 开源 融资 自负盈亏(无轮次披露) 团队 单人 founder 创始人自报、未审计。数字是策略的方向锚,不是财报。 为什么 30 个渠道是护城河 案例点得很清楚。一个 6 渠道的排期工具,处在和其他 6 渠道排期工具的功能对等赛跑里。买家比的是 UI、价格、集成数。工具至少要赢其中一项才能转化。 一个 30 渠道的排期工具不在同一场比赛里。买家把它对标到「雇一个兼职社媒运营,去那 24 个平台手动发」。对标框架从「功能对等」滑到「替代人工」。价格上限从「我愿意为排期工具付多少」跳到「我愿意为不做这些活付多少」。 机械原因是:每个集成是一个第三方 API 加上鉴权流加上格式适配器。集成成本真实存在——一个能用的集成大约 3–10 个工程日。一个 30 渠道的工具代表 90–300 工程日的集成工作。新入场者要花同样的时间才能追平。每多接一个渠道,护城河就厚一分。 开源怎么变成付费漏斗 案例描述了一个三步循环: 开源仓库。 GitHub star 累积起来,反哺可见度。自部署用户装上开始用。 自部署用户撞上运维极限。 一些用户规模超过自部署的舒适区(数据库备份、可用性、版本升级、安全补丁)。他们开始找托管版。 托管版就是付费产品。 自部署用户是预审过的线索,因为他们已经在工作流里验证过产品。转化率结构性地高于冷流量。 案例说得很直白:开源不是慷慨。开源是一条免费的分销渠道,把预审过的线索交到你手上。托管付费产品是这层之上的变现。 定价锚点 案例暗示 Postiz 定价在 30–80 美金/月。锚的是替代人工,不是同行订阅价。 一个兼职社媒运营在大多数市场 1000–2000 美金/月。Postiz 50 美金/月替代大约每月 10 小时的发布工作。买家比的是 50 美金/月 对 1500 美金/月的人工,30 倍价值比。价格上限结构性地高于任何把锚定在 Buffer 或 Hootsuite 定价上的「排期工具」竞争者。 这和之前法律 AI 案例同形:把价格锚到工具替代的人力成本上,而不是同行软件月费上。价格上限从软件预算跑到 HR 预算。 复用的路径 案例暗示单人 founder 想复用结构,有四步: 挑一个现有玩家只覆盖 5–6 个渠道或工作流的品类。 自己覆盖 15+ 长尾。品类不一定

title: Postiz case: 145,000 USD MRR from 30+ social channels and an open-source spine — a builder''s playbook for leaving the mainstream lane
description: Cold read of Postiz (open-source social scheduling tool) crossing 145,000 USD MRR. What it actually monetizes, where its moat sits, what the source quietly skips, and the unit economics of going long-tail instead of competing for mainstream channels.
excerpt: A knowledge-planet post calls out a familiar mistake: builders of tool products start by trying to s
body: A knowledge-planet post calls out a familiar mistake: builders of tool products start by trying to ship "a better version" of an existing tool, which drops them into the most crowded lane competing for the same pool of buyers as the incumbent. The case walks through an open-source social-scheduling tool built by an indie developer that reportedly crossed 145,000 USD MRR by doing something different: it shipped integrations to 30+ publishing channels, including the long-tail ones mainstream tools refuse to integrate. Below is the math, what is load-bearing, and what the case is silent on. What the case describes Quantity Value (self-reported) Product Postiz (open-source social scheduling) Reported MRR 145,000 USD Integrations 30+ (mainstream + long-tail: Mastodon, Bluesky, Nostr, Warpcast, Telegram, Discord, etc.) Licensing Open source + hosted paid plans Architecture Single indie developer (per the case framing) All numbers are founder-attributed. Treat them as advisory; the case uses them to argue the open-source-plus-long-tail combination is the load-bearing strategy, not the feature set. Why 30+ integrations is the moat A feature-style moat (smarter UI, cleaner dashboard, better

title: Product Pricing Calculator for Cross-border Sellers
description: Free Shopify pricing calculator. Enter cost, shipping, fees and target margin — get the suggested price, profit per unit and break-even price instantly.
excerpt: The Product Pricing Calculator answers the question every cross-border seller asks: what should I ch
body: The Product Pricing Calculator answers the question every cross-border seller asks: what should I charge to actually make money? Most pricing formulas stop at "cost × markup." That misses shipping, platform commission, payment processing, and the small per-order fixed fees that quietly eat your margin. This calculator includes all four. How to use it Enter your product cost (what you pay the supplier, in USD). Add your shipping cost per order (or use 0 for free-shipping models). Enter commission % (Shopify Payments is 2.9% in the US; Etsy is 6.5% + 3%; Amazon is 15%). Enter payment processing % (often the same as commission if your platform bundles it). Add any fixed per-order fee (Shopify charges $0.30; PayPal charges $0.49 in some regions). Set your target net margin (after all fees). Read the suggested price, break-even price, and profit per unit. The formula The break-even price assumes 0% net margin — anything you sell above it is profit. Worked example A $8 product with $3.50 shipping, 2.9% commission, 2.9% payment fee, $0.30 fixed fee, and a 30% target margin: Suggested price: $24.85 Break-even price: $13.05 (zero profit) Profit per unit at the suggested price: $7.46 If you

title: Reading the Kimi K3 training timeline: reported 5-month pre-training run, K2.5/K2.6 in between, what changes when a Chinese frontier lab ships on this cadence
description: A reported reader reconstructs the Kimi K3 training timeline from public signals — a December 2025 pre-training start, mid-2026 internal use, July 2026 release, with K2.5/K2.6 post-training enhancements in April and June. Reading the cadence against US frontier labs and the gaps the timeline quietly skips.
excerpt: A reported reader reconstructs the Kimi K3 training timeline from public signals. The version laid o
body: A reported reader reconstructs the Kimi K3 training timeline from public signals. The version laid out: pre-training started in late 2025, completed in roughly 3-4 months, internal use in May 2026, public release in July 2026, with K2.5 and K2.6 post-training enhancements released in April and June. The interesting read is the cadence, not the calendar. The reported timeline Reported milestone Approximate timing Pre-training start December 2025 Pre-training complete March-April 2026 Internal use opens May 2026 K2.5 post-training enhancement release April 2026 K2.6 release June 2026 Public K3 release July 2026 A 5-month pre-training cycle plus a 90-day internal-use-to-public-release window is fast. US frontier teams typically run 6-12 months pre-training plus longer post-training. The reported cadence sits between mid-tier US labs and the largest-scale frontier runs. title: ROAS 保本与目标利润率计算器
description: 根据客单价、成本、支付费率和目标利润率，反算保本 ROAS、目标 ROAS 和最高 CPA。
excerpt: ROAS（广告支出回报率）是付费社交里最常用的指标，但多数卖家只是凭感觉看，不去验算。3.0x ROAS 听起来很好，拆开算利润率——8% 支付费、25% 目标利润率下，保本其实只有 1.85x。低于
body: ROAS（广告支出回报率）是付费社交里最常用的指标，但多数卖家只是凭感觉看，不去验算。3.0x ROAS 听起来很好，拆开算利润率——8% 支付费、25% 目标利润率下，保本其实只有 1.85x。低于这个数，首单就亏钱。 计算器怎么算 客单价 (USD)——售价（打折后实际成交价）。 成本 + 物流 (USD)——到岸价，含生产、包装、运到客户手中的运费。 支付费率 (%)——总处理费，含汇损和平台佣金。 目标利润率 (%)——扣完所有固定成本后想要的净利润率。 输出： 保本 ROAS = 1 ÷ (1 − 支付费率% − 目标利润率%)。低于这个数，每个广告美元都亏。 目标 ROAS——达到目标利润率所需的倍数。 最高 CPA——首单保本情况下你最多能付的获客成本。 参考场景 客单价 成本+物流 支付费率 目标利润率 保本 目标 ROAS 最高 CPA $25 $8 4% 20% 1.32x 1.65x $16.00 $45 $14 4% 25% 1.41x 1.85x $29.20 $80 $22 4% 30% 1.52x 2.16x $54.80 $150 $45 5% 35% 1.67x 2.56x $97.50 $300 $90 5% 40% 1.82x 3.03x $195.00 保本随目标利润率变化慢；最高 CPA 随客单价线性变化。最容易把广告搞崩的杠杆是支付费率漂移（汇损附加、支付商分级调整）——有效费率涨 1%，需要多 10% 的 ROAS 来补偿。 超越 ROAS：四个杠杆 ROAS 是输出。输入的杠杆是： CTR（点击率）——由素材和开场决定。 CVR（转化率）——由落地页、优惠、定价锚点决定。 AOV（客单价）——套装、加购、临界包邮。 CPM（千次曝光成本）——由受众质量和竞争决定。 ROAS 不达标时，按顺序排查：先看 CPM（竞价问题？）、再看 CTR（素材疲劳？）、最后查 CVR（落地页不匹配？）。 常见错误 跨归因窗口比 ROAS——Facebook 7 天点击 vs Google 30 天点击不能直接比。先锁窗口再 benchmark。 忽略退货和拒付——它们让成本涨两次（退货 + 丢失的获客成本）。目标利润率再加 2–5% 吸收它们。 忘记素材疲劳——14 天内 ROAS 跌 30% 但 CPM 没变，多半是素材废了，不是受众废了。 用 LTV 算首单——保本是首单。LTV 数学只在有复购数据时用，否则你在放大不盈利的群组。 把"目标 ROAS"当保证值——它是按你设的利润率算出来的倍数，不是广告实际能跑到的数。 何时这工具不够用 订阅或 LTV 业务——用群组 LTV / CAC 代替首单 ROAS。 高退货率品类（服装、美妆）——把退货率算进利润率假设；20% 退货会吃掉 25% 的目标利润。 B2B 长周期销售—

title: ROAS Break-Even Calculator
description: Compute break-even ROAS, target ROAS, and max CPA from your unit price, cost, payment fee and target margin.
excerpt: ROAS (Return on Ad Spend) is the single most-used metric in paid social, but most sellers eyeball it
body: ROAS (Return on Ad Spend) is the single most-used metric in paid social, but most sellers eyeball it without checking the math. A 3.0x ROAS sounds great until your margins and fees break it down — at 8% payment fee and 25% target margin, your break-even is closer to 1.85x. Anything below that loses money on the first order. How this calculator works Unit price (USD) — selling price (post-discount if you run promos). COGS + shipping (USD) — landed cost including production, packaging, freight to customer. Payment fee (%) — total processor fee including FX and platform commission. Target margin (%) — net margin you want after all fixed costs. The tool returns: Break-even ROAS = 1 / (1 − payment% − margin%). Below this, every ad dollar loses money. Target ROAS — multiplier needed to hit your target margin after ad spend. Max CPA — the most you can pay per acquired customer while staying break-even on order one. Reference scenarios Unit price COGS+ship Payment % Target margin Break-even Target ROAS Max CPA $25 $8 4% 20% 1.32x 1.65x $16.00 $45 $14 4% 25% 1.41x 1.85x $29.20 $80 $22 4% 30% 1.52x 2.16x $54.80 $150 $45 5% 35% 1.67x 2.56x $97.50 $300 $90 5% 40% 1.82x 3.03x $195.00 Break-even

excerpt: Fiverr gigs running on GPT Image (old-photo restoration at $10–25, object removal at $5–25, AI comme
body: Fiverr gigs running on GPT Image (old-photo restoration at $10–25, object removal at $5–25, AI commercial imagery at $20–100+) are a real category with thousands of active sellers. The per-gig math is clean: tool cost in cents, gig price in dollars, and a focused operator can ship 10–30 gigs per day. The margin compresses once dispute / revision rates, platform fees, and the Fiverr churn curve are factored in. Below is the unit economics, the three margin compressors, and the durability question. What the case lays out Category Gig price Tool cost Operator time Skill required Old-photo restoration $10–25 $0.05–0.30 5–15 min Mid Object removal $5–25 $0.05–0.20 3–10 min Low AI commercial imagery $20–100+ $0.20–0.80 10–25 min Mid–High The case frames these as three categories with similar economics. The differences in skill requirement and price ceiling matter more than the table implies — high-skill gigs (commercial imagery) carry lower volume but higher per-gig margin. Per-gig unit economics A focused operator shipping 15 gigs/day at an average $25 price generates roughly $300/day gross, or $7,500/month gross. body: Spott — 3 founders from McKinsey / BCG / Bain, 2024 launch, 8x revenue growth in 6 months to 1M USD/month (12M USD annualized). YC W25, 3.2M USD seed (Base10). Demand is validated, pain is acute, migration barrier locks the win. The unit economics, the migration moat, and the four gaps the case does not cover, below. Why boring old-software replacement is the best AI play The 3 reasons compound. A reader scanning AI plays should weight boring old-software replacement over greenfield AI products because the demand side is solved. The risk is execution, not market. The unit economics at 1M USD/month The pricing math jumps 10–50x compared to incumbent ATS seats. The reason is the anchor — Spott sells against recruiter loaded cost (100K+ USD/year), not against software seat cost (1.2K–3.6K USD/year). title: The "30-day AI product bootcamp" pitch: a unit-economics teardown
description: A line-by-line read of a typical "30-day AI product monetization bootcamp" sales post, what it promises, what it leaves out, and how to decide whether the offer is worth the asking price.
excerpt: The post in question is a sales post for a 30-day AI product bootcamp. The pitch is: from product se
body: The post in question is a sales post for a 30-day AI product bootcamp. The pitch is: from product selection through build to promotion, in 30 days, taught in a cohort format, with a Quark-pan link to the curriculum. The format is familiar to most; this teardown applies to offers of this shape. The question is not whether the curriculum is real. It is whether the outcome the post implies (a "sustainable side-hustle" from a 30-day commitment) is what the curriculum actually delivers. What the pitch says, mapped to deliverables Reading the post literally, the offer promises four components: Product selection. Pick an AI-automation product category where the buyer has clear pain and budget. Build. Use no-code + AI to ship a v0 within 30 days. Promotion. Paid and organic distribution plan with templates. Long-term asset framing. "构建长期副业资产" (build a long-term side-hustle asset). The 30-day window is enough to ship (1) and (2). It is not enough to validate (3) or to demonstrate (4). What 30 days can and cannot do Stage 30-day outcome What it does not cover Selection Pick a category with margin 50% and clear buyer profile Validate that the chosen category has reachable distribution channel

description: A viral X post described a single operator charging $1,000/hour to walk small business owners through a 45-minute audit, identify 5–10 hours of weekly time loss, and prescribe off-the-shelf AI tools to recover them. A read of the math, the conversion funnel, and the failure modes.
excerpt: A viral X post (2,032 likes, 111 replies, 136 reposts) described a single operator charging $1,000/h
body: A viral X post (2,032 likes, 111 replies, 136 reposts) described a single operator charging $1,000/hour for a 45-minute audit of small business workflows, identifying 5–10 hours of weekly time loss, and prescribing off-the-shelf AI tools to recover them. The implied premise — that "only 5% of businesses use AI beyond ChatGPT, the other 95% need you" — points to a real demand pool, but the math, the funnel, and the failure modes need to be drawn out before anyone copies the model. The shape of the engagement Stage Duration Deliverable Buyer pays for Discovery call 45 min Time-loss map, 3–7 tool recommendations $750 (or $1,000/hour equivalent) Implementation 4–12 hr Tools configured, team trained Often separately quoted Ongoing retainer Monthly Tool updates, new audits, troubleshooting $500–2,000/month The post implies the audit is the headline product. The follow-on implementation and retainer are where most of the recurring revenue sits, but they are not what the post is selling. Why $1,000/hour is anchored to a real reference price The buyer is comparing the $750 audit against two reference prices. title: The 17M USD a year an AI photo app kept by switching its payment rail off the App Store
description: A small AI photo-and-video team reportedly grew 2025 revenue 14x to 55M USD annualized, then to 70M USD. Same users, same product, different checkout. The 30% Apple cut is the lever most operators forget to pull.
excerpt: Source This case is a paraphrased summary of publicly reported coverage of an unnamed AI photo-and-v
body: Source This case is a paraphrased summary of publicly reported coverage of an unnamed AI photo-and-video app team and adjacent web-checkout case studies. The headline figures (55M USD annualized → 70M USD annualized, 14x year-over-year growth, 17M USD/year implied gross retained by moving off the App Store) are quoted as the source material reports them; none are independently audited by the editor. Apple's standard 30% App Store fee, the small-business 15% program, and the typical 2.9% + 0.30 USD Stripe/Paddle checkout rates are public pricing pages. This post is commentary on payment-rail economics, not on a specific company; if you can point to a specific original report we should credit by name, write to contact@kuajinglab.xyz.

excerpt: A practical AI-tool account reportedly reached about 22,000 followers by publishing Codex and Claude
body: A practical AI-tool account reportedly reached about 22,000 followers by publishing Codex and Claude Code tutorials and prompt examples. One post reached 15,000 likes and more than 1.5 million views. Monetization came from a 19.9 RMB “Codex notes” product that sold 169 copies, plus paid remote-assistance work. The arithmetic turns the story from “viral tutorials” into a small product-to-service funnel. The disclosed funnel math Metric Reported or derived Followers 22,000 Peak likes on one post 15,000 Peak views on one post 1.5M+ Notebook price 19.9 RMB Notebook sales 169 Notebook gross revenue 3,363.10 RMB Sales ÷ followers 0.77% Gross revenue per follower 0.153 RMB The 0.77% ratio is not a clean conversion rate. Some buyers may not follow the account, many followers may never have seen the offer, and the follower count may have grown after the sales period. It is still a useful scale check. A 22,000-follower account did not turn a 19.9 RMB file into a large standalone business. The disclosed file revenue is 3,363.10 RMB before fees and support. That does not make the file a bad product. It changes its job. The notebook can turn broad tutorial attention into a list of 169 people wh

title: The 200 USD Bing referral click: why the reported ad math contradicts itself
description: A reported AI-assisted Bing Ads referral model claims 200 USD per approved card referral, 0.50–2 USD clicks, and monthly income from brand-keyword traffic—but its volume assumptions do not reconcile.
excerpt: A reported paid-search model buys Bing traffic for financial-product brand queries and earns about 2
body: A reported paid-search model buys Bing traffic for financial-product brand queries and earns about 200 USD when a card referral succeeds. AI writes ad variants, suggests keyword expansions, and summarizes campaign data. The case says the operator averages 8–12 referrals per month, implying 1,600–2,400 USD monthly revenue. It then gives a second example that claims 50 clicks per day on a 10 USD budget and one 200 USD conversion per day. Those numbers do not fit together. The two sets of numbers Input Reported recurring result Separate “conservative” example Reward per successful referral 200 USD 200 USD Successful referrals 8–12/month 1/day, or 30/month Monthly revenue 1,600–2,400 USD 6,000 USD Daily ad budget Not disclosed 10 USD Claimed daily clicks Not disclosed 50 Claimed conversion Not disclosed 2% Claimed monthly ad spend Not disclosed 300 USD The recurring result is internally simple: The second example fails at the click-cost line. Fifty daily clicks on a 10 USD budget requires an average CPC of 0.20 USD. Yet the same case gives a starting bid of 0.50 USD and describes clicks as costing from several tenths to 1–2 USD. title: The 3,548 USD AI virtual-influencer month: 90 videos, three platforms, and the missing revenue mix
description: A reported 22-year-old operator built an AI virtual performer and earned 3,548 USD in one month after publishing three videos per day across TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube.
excerpt: A reported 22-year-old operator in Japan created a virtual performer named Lin, framed as a Chinese
body: A reported 22-year-old operator in Japan created a virtual performer named Lin, framed as a Chinese woman who sings and dances. Claude helped define the character, generative tools produced the videos, and the operator posted three times per day across TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube. The reported first-month revenue was 3,548 USD. That is specific enough to test the publishing math, but not enough to know whether the account was profitable. The publishing and revenue math Item Reported or derived Operator age 22 Platforms 3 Publishing frequency 3 videos per day 30-day publishing slots 90 First-month revenue 3,548 USD Revenue per daily slot 39.42 USD Annualized run rate, if stable 42,576 USD The 90-slot figure needs care. “Three videos per day across three platforms” could mean three unique videos posted to all platforms, one unique video cross-posted three times, or another mix. If the operator made one unique video per day and published it on three platforms, the month contained 30 original assets and 90 distribution events. Revenue per original asset would then be 118.27 USD. If there were 90 unique assets, it would be 39.42 USD each. Neither number proves repeatability. Creator

title: The Cross-border Pricing Formula (with Worked Examples)
description: The exact formula cross-border sellers use to price products on Shopify, Etsy and Amazon. Includes worked examples for dropshipping, branded products and high-AOV niches.
excerpt: Most sellers price by gut feel: cost × 3 sounds right. That works until the third month when the boo
body: Most sellers price by gut feel: cost × 3 sounds right. That works until the third month when the books show a loss. Here's the formula professional cross-border sellers actually use. The formula Where: cost = supplier price in USD shipping = per-order shipping cost to customer fixed fees = per-order platform fees (Shopify $0.30, PayPal $0.49 in some markets) commission + payment = combined percentage fee target margin = net profit % you want to keep Worked example 1 — branded Shopify product Cost: $8 Shipping: $3.50 Shopify Payments fee: 2.9% + $0.30 Target margin: 35% Profit per unit: $19.00 × (1 − 0.029) − 11.80 = $6.65 Worked example 2 — Etsy handmade Cost: $12 (materials + labor) Shipping: $5 (with $1 packaging) Etsy transaction fee: 6.5% Etsy payment processing: 3% + $0.25 Target margin: 40% Worked example 3 — Amazon FBA Cost: $6 Amazon referral fee: 15% FBA fulfillment: $4.50 Target margin: 25% The break-even price (0% margin) is $13.13. Anything above that is profit, anything below is a loss. Why markup-based pricing fails A 3× markup on $8 gives $24. But: Item Amount Revenue $24.00 Shopify fee (2.9% + $0.30) −$1.00 Payment processing (already included above) — Shipping −$3.

title: The Math of a 5K USD/Month Solo AI SaaS
description: How many paying users a one-person AI SaaS actually needs to clear 5K USD/mo. The LTV/CAC math behind three typical price points.
excerpt: A solo AI SaaS that replaces a salary needs to clear roughly 5K USD/month MRR. The number of paying
body: A solo AI SaaS that replaces a salary needs to clear roughly 5K USD/month MRR. The number of paying users that requires depends on three things: monthly price, customer acquisition cost, average customer lifetime. Plug in real numbers and the conclusion is uncomfortable: at typical solo-founder price points, you need many more users than the founding story suggests. The setup A solo founder, no employees, no sales team. Distribution through content, community, and one-time launches (Product Hunt, X, indie communities). No paid ads. A product that is genuinely useful — churn is the natural rate for the category, not artificially inflated by bad UX. Plug into the break-even calculator: Price CAC Avg lifetime Payback users Monthly MRR at payback 9 USD/mo 40 USD 6 months 27 243 USD 29 USD/mo 80 USD 8 months 23 667 USD 99 USD/mo 200 USD 12 months 25 2,475 USD Payback user count is roughly the same across price points (20-30 users) — the math is forgiving at the lower end. The painful number is the MRR at the payback point, which is price multiplied by user count. Hitting 5K USD MRR requires scaling 8-20x past break-even. What 5K USD MRR actually requires Price Required paying users Requ

title: The new AI bottleneck is electricity, not chips: a tweet-thread read on power-grid timing, hyperscaler capex, and the unit-economics shift
description: A reported investor argument moves the AI-supply bottleneck from chips to electricity. Power-grid buildout cadence, hyperscaler capex passthrough, and the unit-economics shift this argument quietly assumes.
excerpt: An investor argument moves the AI-supply bottleneck from chips to electricity. Chips still lead the
body: An investor argument moves the AI-supply bottleneck from chips to electricity. Chips still lead the dollar-weight in capex; the bottleneck has simply slipped down the stack to a layer that builds slower. The argument is directionally credible; it is also quantifiably thin. The thread gives none of the numbers a serious read would need to test it. What the argument actually claims Three claims stacked into one paragraph: Chip supply has loosened at the training frontier. Data-center power demand is growing faster than grid buildout. Per-token economics will shift as passthrough arrives. Each is independently testable. The case treats them as a settled sequence without giving baselines. The cadence gap Resource Buildout cadence Why it matters Leading-edge GPU equivalent 9-12 months from wafer to deployment Chip bottleneck eased in 2024-2025 HBM (high-bandwidth memory) 12-18 months from fab to validated stack Still tight through 2025-2026 Grid interconnection (US, large load) 4-7 years from application to energization Binding input behind the chip layer Behind-the-meter gas turbine 18-36 months from order to commissioning Workaround, not a wholesale answer Small modular reactor (SMR)

title: TikTok Shop 佣金拆解：创作者和卖家到底付多少
description: TikTok Shop 2026 年的每一笔费用：推荐佣金、交易服务费、创作者分成、运费补贴规则、90 天退货窗口。$30 产品完整算账。
excerpt: TikTok Shop 的费率结构是主流电商平台里最短的，但惩罚那些不把创作者分成算进去的卖家。下面是一款 $30 产品的真实成本。 $30 产品的费用堆栈 费用 金额 占售价比 售价 $30.00
body: TikTok Shop 的费率结构是主流电商平台里最短的，但惩罚那些不把创作者分成算进去的卖家。下面是一款 $30 产品的真实成本。 $30 产品的费用堆栈 费用 金额 占售价比 售价 $30.00 100% 推荐佣金（6%，服饰类） $1.80 6% 交易服务费（1.8%） $0.54 1.8% TikTok 抽成 $2.34 7.8% 创作者分成（10% 常见） $3.00 10% TikTok 从创作者分成抽的 30% $0.90 3% 创作者 + TikTok 分成层 $3.90 13% 平台 + 创作者合计 $6.24 20.8% 这是底线。叠上 5% 退货率、10% 站外广告、$0.30 固定交易成本，画面就变了。 各品类推荐佣金 品类 推荐费 % 服饰（衣服、配件） 6% 家居 6% 电子 4% 美妆 5% 宠物用品 5% 玩具与收藏 5% 图书与媒体 4% 食品饮料 4% 汽车配件 4% 比 Amazon 15% 推荐费低得多。但创作者分成才是算账不严时的暴雷点。 创作者分成——隐形成本 大多数 TikTok Shop 卖家依赖创作者。卖家自己设佣金率（5–20% 常见），任何带你这件货的创作者都按这个比例拿销售分成。 创作者带一单时： 创作者得：佣金率 × 售价（如 10% × $30 = $3.00） TikTok 得：创作者所得的 30%（$3.00 × 30% = $0.90） 10% 的创作者分成实际成本是营收的 13%，不是 10%。 思路：把分成当营销成本算 把创作者分成当营销支出，不当平台费。 $30 产品、$9 成本、10% 创作者分成、8% 退货率、15% 广告费： 仍然健康，前提是创作者转化率 4% 以上。低于这个，60%+ 毛利会被分成层吃掉变成个位数。 退货窗口与成本 TikTok Shop 有 90 天退货窗口。大部分品类要求卖家付退货运费： 标准运费标签：$3.00–$4.50 超大件：$6.00–$9.00 $30 产品 10% 退货率，$4 退货运费，每卖一件平均被吃 $1.20，还没算仓储与重新上架。 新卖家激励 TikTok Shop 跑 90 天新卖家计划： 90 天 0% 推荐佣金（品类不同） 30 天 0% 交易服务费 最多 $1,500 创作者撮合 $200 广告金 相当于第一个月平台抽成为 0%，第二三个月降到 1.8%，然后跳回 6%+1.8%。上线量和促销后定价要按这个规划。 FBT（TikTok 发货）vs 卖家自发货 FBT 卖家自发货 履约费 $2.50–$4.50/件（按尺寸） 视情况 运费补贴 无要求 卖家吸收或转嫁 退货处理 $1.50/件 $3.00+/件 仓储 前 100 件免费，超出 $0.40/立方英尺/月 无 2 日达标志 有 无 FBT 提升算法排名，但加 $2.50

title: TikTok Shop Commission Breakdown: What Creators and Sellers Actually Pay
description: Every fee TikTok Shop charges in 2026: referral commission, transaction fee, creator affiliate cut, shipping subsidy rules, and the 90-day return window. With a worked example for a $30 product.
excerpt: TikTok Shop's fee structure is the shortest among major marketplaces, but it punishes sellers who do
body: TikTok Shop's fee structure is the shortest among major marketplaces, but it punishes sellers who do not model the creator affiliate cut. Here is the real cost on a $30 product. Fee stack on a $30 product Fee Amount % of price Sale price $30.00 100% Referral commission (6%, fashion) $1.80 6% Transaction fee (1.8%) $0.54 1.8% TikTok's cut $2.34 7.8% Creator affiliate (10% typical) $3.00 10% TikTok's cut of creator fee (30%) $0.90 3% Creator + TikTok affiliate layer $3.90 13% Total platform + creator $6.24 20.8% That is the floor. Add a 5% return rate, 10% offsite ad spend, and a $0.30 fixed transaction cost, and the picture changes. Referral commission by category Category Referral % Fashion (apparel, accessories) 6% Home & living 6% Electronics 4% Health & beauty 5% Pet supplies 5% Toys & collectibles 5% Books & media 4% Food & beverage 4% Auto accessories 4% Compared to Amazon's 15% referral, TikTok Shop's commission is one-third. But the creator affiliate layer is what makes the math unforgiving if you skip the modeling. Creator affiliate — the hidden cost Most TikTok Shop sellers rely on creators. title: Time-zone arbitrage: a reported MIT student running three hours a night cleared 4.5M USD — the math behind the headline, and what the case skips
description: A reported timezone-arbitrage workflow has an MIT student answering prompts in the gap between Asia and US working hours to clear 4.5M USD. The unit economics, the role of the LLM, and what the case quietly skips.
excerpt: A reported workflow: an MIT student runs three overnight hours and clears 4.5M USD by operating in t
body: A reported workflow: an MIT student runs three overnight hours and clears 4.5M USD by operating in the timezone gap between Asia's end of day and the US morning. The AI in the loop is ChatGPT for ideation and Gemini for execution. The headline works out to roughly 11.5K USD per working night over the years it would take to reach that cumulative figure, which is uncomfortable enough that a careful read should treat the number as a claim, not a fact. The cadence, in numbers Input Reported value What it caps Working hours per night 3 Operator focus Working nights per week 5 Cadence Working weeks per year 50 Holiday buffer Annual capacity 750 hours Hard deliverable ceiling Cumulative 4.5M USD over say 4 years 1.125M USD/year 1,500 USD/hour minimum At 750 hours per year and 1,500 USD/hour, the operator clears 1.125M USD/year. The 4.5M USD cumulative figure implies four years at that level, with no compounding, no team, no leverage. That is possible but rare; it requires either premium consulting rates or productized flows that scale with AI throughput rather than operator hours. Where the 4.5M USD plausibly comes from Revenue model Fit with the case Revenue shape Premium US consulting M

title: Toonflow 一句话出一部完整短剧:2026 年 6 agent AI 视频制作的单位经济
description: Toonflow,一个 AI 短剧流水线。输入:一句话。输出:完整短剧——剧本、分镜、配音、剪辑全自动。流水线跑 6 个 AI agent(导演、编剧、分镜、质检、配音、剪辑)像真实剧组一样分工。头条特性:跨镜头角色一致——AI 视频最难的问题。这条不是「AI 视频有效」——是 2026 年 6 agent AI 短剧制作的单位经济,以及「角色一致性」突破究竟解锁了什么。
excerpt: 一条推文拆的是 Toonflow——一个 AI 短剧流水线,接一句一句话输入,出一部完整短剧(剧本、分镜、配音、剪辑全自动)。流水线跑 6 个 AI agent(导演、编剧、分镜、配音、质检、剪辑)像
body: 一条推文拆的是 Toonflow——一个 AI 短剧流水线,接一句一句话输入,出一部完整短剧(剧本、分镜、配音、剪辑全自动)。流水线跑 6 个 AI agent(导演、编剧、分镜、配音、质检、剪辑)像真实剧组一样分工。用户不用配环境、不用写 prompt;界面是拖拽分镜、调台词、换 BGM。头条技术声称:跨镜头、跨场景角色一致性——AI 视频最难的问题。重点不是「AI 视频有效」,是:2026 年 6 agent AI 短剧制作的单位经济,以及「角色一致性」突破究竟对海外短剧市场解锁了什么。下面是单集数学、角色一致性突破、以及案例没展开的四点。 6 agent 流水线 导演 agent 把角色规范传给所有下游 agent。质检 agent 验证一致性。用户通过拖拽在分镜阶段介入,其余自动化。流水线用模型推理成本替代 5–10 人剧组。 角色一致性突破 AI 视频自 2023 年以来最难的问题是角色一致性。之前的模型每个镜头都把角色画得稍微不一样——脸、衣服、身材都不同——打破沉浸感。6 agent 流水线声称解决: 突破不在模型——在编排。导演 agent 的规范 + 质检 agent 的验证,是给跨镜头一致性的东西。案例是公开声称 6 agent 流水线跨镜头一致性的首例。独立基准还没验证。 单位经济 案例没给单集成本。隐含 5–50 美金。AI 成本压缩相对传统制作(单 70 集 5K–50K)10–100 倍。瓶颈不是成本;瓶颈是分发。 为什么角色一致性是解锁 角色一致性问题在 2026 年是 AI 短剧采用最大的单一阻塞。operator 能便宜出 70 集,但镜头之间主角脸变,观众不容忍,平台不推。 如果 Toonflow 的角色一致性声明成立,解锁是体量游戏。operator 一周能出 1–3 部,接近零生产成本,知道每部都沉浸稳定。72 小时海外营收曲线按周复利。复利是解锁。 如果声明不成立,Toonflow 是个产出 70 集沉浸破碎内容的工具。买家(operator)在质检里发现这事,因为制作成本已沉,还是出货,看 72 小时曲线表现不及预期。质检环节是隐性风险。 案例没说的事 四点。 单集成本。 案例没给美元数。隐含 5–50 美金。 失败模式。 6 agent 流水线产出不一致镜头时怎么办;用户手动覆盖有限。案例没处理质检 agent 标记一个镜头时用户做什么。 质量上限。 案例框定 6 agent 编排是突破,但没处理输出质量的上限(电影级短剧 vs 竖屏短剧)。6 agent 流水线针对竖屏格式优化;电影级是另一回事。 分发 CAC。 案例没处理拉到付费观众的成本;生产成本是单位经济的小头。案例忽略分发瓶颈。 结论 重点不是「Toonflow 能做短剧」,是:一个解决角色一致性的 6 agent AI 视频流水线,是 AI 短剧体量

title: Toonflow takes one sentence and outputs a full short drama: the unit economics of 6-agent AI video production in 2026
description: A look at Toonflow, an AI short-drama pipeline. Input: one sentence. Output: a complete short drama — script, storyboard, voice, edit, all automated. The pipeline runs 6 AI agents (director, screenwriter, storyboard, QC, voice) that divide work like a real production team. The headline feature: character consistency across shots, which is the hardest problem in AI video. The case is not 'AI video works'. The case is: the unit economics of 6-agent AI drama production in 2026, and what the character-consistency breakthrough actually unlocks.
excerpt: A case profiles Toonflow, an AI short-drama pipeline that takes a one-sentence input and outputs a c
body: A case profiles Toonflow, an AI short-drama pipeline that takes a one-sentence input and outputs a complete short drama — script, storyboard, voice, edit, all automated. The pipeline runs 6 AI agents (director, screenwriter, storyboard, voice, QC, editor) that divide work like a real production team. The user does not need to set up environments or write prompts; the interface is drag-and-drop storyboard, dialogue editing, BGM replacement. The headline technical claim: character consistency across shots and scenes, which is the hardest problem in AI video. The case is not 'AI video works'. The case is: the unit economics of 6-agent AI drama production in 2026, and what the character-consistency breakthrough actually unlocks for the overseas short-drama market. Below is the per-episode math, the character-consistency breakthrough, and four gaps the case does not address. The 6-agent pipeline The director agent passes the character spec to every downstream agent. The QC agent validates consistency. The user intervenes at the storyboard stage via drag-and-drop; the rest is automated. The pipeline replaces a 5–10 person production team at the cost of model inference. The character-cons

title: Two guys, $400M in telehealth sales, 250K patients, no doctors hired: why 'healthcare company' is the wrong frame and 'patient-acquisition layer' is the right one
description: A look at Matthew Gallagher's telehealth company. 2024-09: launch with $20K and no employees. 2025-end: $400M in sales, 250,000 patients. 2026: on track for $1.8B. Total headcount: 2 (Gallagher and his brother). The interesting part: Gallagher did not build a healthcare company. He built a customer-acquisition and experience layer on top of someone else's infrastructure. Doctors are subcontracted, licenses are rented from a partner, pharmacy is outsourced, logistics is outsourced. The company owns three things: brand, distribution, customer relationship. The lesson here is not a healthcare insight but a customer-acquisition insight.
excerpt: A case profiles a telehealth company launched by Matthew Gallagher in September 2024 with $20,000 of
body: A case profiles a telehealth company launched by Matthew Gallagher in September 2024 with $20,000 of capital and zero employees. By end-2025: $400M in sales, 250,000 patients served. By 2026: on track for $1.8B. Total headcount: 2 — Gallagher and his brother. The interesting part is not the healthcare insight (what condition is being treated, what drug is being prescribed) but the structural one: Gallagher did not build a healthcare company. He built a customer-acquisition and experience layer on top of someone else's healthcare infrastructure. Doctors are subcontracted. Pharmacy is outsourced. Logistics is outsourced. Licenses are rented from a partner. The company itself owns three things: brand, distribution, customer relationship. The unit economics of a customer-acquisition layer are tech-grade, not healthcare-grade. Per-patient math, the asset-light structure, and four gaps the case does not address, below." The asset-light structure The owned components are: brand, performance marketing, customer portal, billing, customer support, retention. title: Vertical AI vs General AI: Why the Next Wave of Independent AI Tools Is Not a Wrapper on GPT
description: A directional note from a Chinese knowledge-planet thread: the general-purpose AI tool category is saturated, but vertical AI tools in legaltech, medtech, real estate, education and cross-border ecommerce are under-built. Cold-eyed read of which verticals actually pay, which are compliance quagmires, and where the indie operator can enter.
excerpt: A directional note from a Chinese knowledge-planet thread: "In the AI-for-export direction, do we be
body: A directional note from a Chinese knowledge-planet thread: "In the AI-for-export direction, do we bet on the general-tool traffic play, or do we go deep into a vertical?" Five candidate verticals (legaltech, medtech, real estate, education, cross-border ecommerce). The case argues that the general-AI tool category is saturated but vertical AI is "not yet fully developed." Where each vertical actually pays. What the case actually argues A market-direction note, not a case study. Three observations: General AI categories (writing, translation, design) are "extremely crowded." Vertical AI categories (legaltech, medtech, real estate, education, cross-border ecommerce) look under-built. Compliance burden and customer acquisition cost vary sharply by vertical. The case invites readers to share real case studies. Below I treat each vertical individually rather than treating the note as if it were evidence. The five verticals: where the math actually works Legaltech — contract review, compliance checks Dimension Reading Willingness to pay High. A missed clause costs the customer more than the entire annual tool fee. Compliance burden High but for the customer, not the operator. The operato

title: Why Gamma hit 100M USD ARR and Tome shut down in the same AI-PPT race
description: Two AI-PPT products, one outcome of 100M USD ARR with 100 people at 2.1B USD valuation, the other shut down in April 2025 despite 32M USD raised. The split is a product-intuition story, not a model-performance story. Cold read of the numbers and what a solo builder can take from it.
excerpt: Two AI-PPT products competed in the same window. One hit 100M USD ARR with under 100 people at a 2.1
body: Two AI-PPT products competed in the same window. One hit 100M USD ARR with under 100 people at a 2.1B USD valuation. The other shut down in April 2025 after raising 32M USD. Below is a cold read of why one worked and the other did not, plus a checklist for a solo builder looking at the same niche. The two numbers Company Outcome Window Capital Headcount Gamma 100M USD ARR, 2.1B USD valuation Launch 2022, ARR target end-2025 Not named <100 Tome Product shut down 30 April 2025 32M USD raised Not named The contrast is the entire case. Same niche. Same calendar window. Different outcomes by an order of magnitude on revenue and a full wipe on the loser side. What Gamma actually sells The case is direct: Gamma sells layout and styling, not content. The user types the words. Gamma handles the slide structure, the visual rhythm, the image placement, and the export. The job-to-be-done is "I know what I want to say, I do not want to spend two hours saying it in PowerPoint". This positioning drives a weekly cadence. People who make decks weekly (consultants, founders, PMs, sales engineers, internal comms people) come back. title: Why the AI trade has roughly four more quarters before the IPO wave forces a repricing
description: A public timeline argument for when AI demand softens, framed by the gap between the OpenAI / Anthropic IPO window and the discount private investors accept to get in earlier.
excerpt: The argument running through one widely-shared post is timing, not technology. The claim is that US
body: The argument running through one widely-shared post is timing, not technology. The claim is that US AI demand stays elevated through roughly Q4 2026 because the exit path for private AI capital is still private. The minute OpenAI or Anthropic actually IPOs, the discount that has kept late-stage private rounds attractive collapses, and the trade-cycle break shows up before the model quality declines. That is a statement about capital structure, not about what GPT-5 can or cannot do. The IPO window as a clock If we take the public Q4 2026 estimate for OpenAI and the parallel Q4 2026 to mid-2027 estimate for Anthropic at face value, the visible exit for late-stage AI capital is, at most, four quarters out. Either company slipping that timeline by a quarter or two does not change the shape of the trade; it pushes the inflection point right by an equivalent amount. Until the first IPO prints, three things stay true: Private secondary tender offers remain the only exit for most late funds. Pricing those offers at 30-50% below the last primary mark has been observed in 2024-2025 secondaries for several AI names; the size of that discount reflects who is left writing the check, not the und

title: Why the keyword 'how to make money with AI' itself is the tell: a short note on AI-monetization supply vs scenario scarcity in 2026
description: A case profiles a short operator note: 'AI monetization is the most profitable method' is itself a trending keyword. The operator's read: tools are not scarce, scenarios where AI output can be sold are. A 200-word observation, with the supply-side vs demand-side breakdown, what 'scenario scarcity' actually means in 2026, and the four gaps the observation quietly elides.
excerpt: A short operator note: 'AI monetization is the most profitable method' is itself a trending keyword.
body: A short operator note: 'AI monetization is the most profitable method' is itself a trending keyword. The operator's read: tools are not scarce, prompts are not scarce, scenarios where AI output can be sold are. The interesting part is not 'AI is amazing' — it is the supply-side vs demand-side imbalance. The scenario-scarcity framing, where the scarce scenarios actually are in 2026, and the four gaps the observation elides, below. The supply-side vs demand-side split The supply-side grew 10–50x in three years; the demand side grew 2–3x. The imbalance is the operator's observation. The supply-side glut pushes tools and prompts to commodity pricing; the demand-side scarcity keeps scenario-specific revenue intact. Where the scarce scenarios are in 2026 Four categories, each with a different buyer, a different unit economics, a different platform-policy risk. The scarcity is not 'AI can do it' — it is 'a buyer exists who will pay for it'. Why scenarios are scarce Three forces keep the demand side tight: Buyer trust. Most buyers will not pay for AI-generated output they can run themselves with a 20 USD/month subscription. The buyer pays because they do not have the time, the tool access,
